extra.reasoning:
The safety invariant is "opted_out implies not sent" (never opted_out=true AND sent=true at the same time). It is an inductive invariant preserved by every transition, so it holds on all reachable executions regardless of the 7-event bound.

The only transition that sets sent:=true is [t_send_allowed] on send_attempt, guarded by "not opted_out". Thus at the instant sent becomes true, opted_out is necessarily false. The only transition that sets opted_out:=true is [t_optout] on optout, and that same transition atomically sets sent:=false. Hence opted_out can never flip to true while sent is true — it always clears sent in the same step.

Checking preservation case by case from any state satisfying the invariant: (1) optout -> opted_out=true but sent=false, holds; (2) send_attempt while opted_out -> [t_send_blocked] sets sent=false, holds; (3) send_attempt while not opted_out -> sent=true but opted_out=false, holds; (4) any key_0/key_1/key_2 -> only changes stage and sets sent=false, holds. The initial state (opted_out=false, sent=false) trivially satisfies the invariant.

The combination-lock machinery (stage 0..4, adv_*/rst_* via key_0/key_1/key_2) is irrelevant to the property: no transition ever sets sent:=true based on reaching stage==4 (or any stage); reaching the final stage does not enable a send. Every key transition sets sent:=false. So an opted-out send can never go through. Property holds; no counterexample exists.
